chapter.title: Overview
chapter.para: Writing a Dory-Python extension is a fairly straight-forward process. One simply imports the Dory module from the gobject introspection repository and creates a class which is derived from a gobject.GObject and one of the Dory module's classes. When an extension derives a class, it becomes a "provider", telling Dory to ask it for information. There are several types of providers available for extensions to use: there is MenuProvider, LocationWidgetProvider, ColumnProvider, PropertyPageProvider, and InfoProvider, all of which will be explained in more detail below. Your class can be derived from multiple providers.
1. A script is written and installed to the standard dory-python extension install path
2. Dory is (re)started and loads the dory-python C extension, which in turn loads all python extensions it can find

3. Any python script in the standard dory-python extension install path that imports the Dory module from the gobject introspection repository and derives the main class from a Dory module class will be loaded

note.title: A note about the standard python extensions install path
note.para: As of dory-python 0.7.0 (and continued in 1.0+), dory-python looks in ~/.local/share/dory-python/extensions for local extensions and $PREFIX/share/dory-python/extensions for global extensions.

note.title: A note about compatibility issues for dory-python 1.0
note.para: 1. We no longer support the "import dory" command that previous versions used. This is because dory-python now directly uses Dory's annotations, which must be imported by the "from gi.repository import Dory" command.
2. The extension class must now derive itself from a gobject.GObject in addition to the standard Dory classes.
3. For now, some Dory class constructors require passing named arguments instead of a standard argument list. This requirement may go away at some point.
